purpose: This decision tree helps determine the necessary steps and legal requirements for transferring property ownership in Sri Lanka.
entry_question: Is the property registered under the Land Registry?

start.question: Is the property registered under the Land Registry?

node_2.question: Is there a clear title with no encumbrances?

node_3.question: Has a deed of transfer been prepared by a licensed notary?

node_4.question: Has the deed been duly signed and witnessed?

outcome_1.explanation: Unregistered properties require registration before transfer.
outcome_1.next_steps: Initiate the registration process under the Registration of Documents Ordinance.

outcome_2.explanation: A clear title is necessary to proceed with the transfer.
outcome_2.next_steps: Resolve any encumbrances or disputes regarding the title.

outcome_3.explanation: A deed of transfer is required to legally transfer property.
outcome_3.next_steps: Engage a licensed notary to prepare the deed of transfer.

outcome_4.explanation: All legal requirements for transferring the property have been met.
outcome_4.next_steps: Submit the deed for registration at the Land Registry.

outcome_5.explanation: The deed must be signed and witnessed to be valid.
outcome_5.next_steps: Ensure the deed is signed by all parties and witnessed by two individuals.

purpose: Determine if a property is eligible for title registration under the Bim Saviya program
entry_question: Is the property currently registered under the old deed system?

start.question: Is the property currently registered under the old deed system?
start.legal_basis: Registration of Title Act, No. 21 of 1998

node_2.question: Is the property free from any disputes or encumbrances?
node_2.legal_basis: Registration of Title Act, No. 21 of 1998, Section 14

node_3.question: Has the property owner applied for conversion to the title registration system?
node_3.legal_basis: Registration of Title Act, No. 21 of 1998, Section 5

outcome_1.result: Property is not eligible for title registration
outcome_1.explanation: The property must be registered under the old deed system to be eligible for conversion.
outcome_1.next_steps: Consider registering the property under the old deed system first.

outcome_2.result: Property is not eligible for title registration
outcome_2.explanation: Properties with disputes or encumbrances cannot be converted to the title registration system.
outcome_2.next_steps: Resolve any disputes or encumbrances before applying for title registration.

outcome_3.result: Property is eligible for title registration
outcome_3.explanation: The property is registered under the old deed system, free from disputes, and the owner has applied for conversion.
outcome_3.next_steps: Proceed with the title registration process under the Bim Saviya program.

outcome_4.result: Property is not yet eligible for title registration
outcome_4.explanation: The property owner has not applied for conversion to the title registration system.
outcome_4.next_steps: Submit an application for conversion to the title registration system.

purpose: Determine if a person can claim ownership of property through prescription or adverse possession in Sri Lanka.
entry_question: Has the person been in continuous and uninterrupted possession of the property for 10 years?

start.question: Has the person been in continuous and uninterrupted possession of the property for 10 years?

node_2.question: Was the possession open and notorious?
node_2.legal_basis: Common Law Principle of Adverse Possession

node_3.question: Was the possession without the consent of the true owner?

node_4.question: Has the possession been exclusive?
node_4.legal_basis: Common Law Principle of Adverse Possession

outcome_1.explanation: The person has not met the minimum requirement of 10 years of possession.
outcome_1.next_steps: Consider other legal avenues or continue possession until the 10-year requirement is met.

outcome_2.result: Cannot claim ownership through adverse possession.
outcome_2.explanation: Possession was not open and notorious, failing a key requirement.
outcome_2.next_steps: Ensure possession is open and notorious and reassess after meeting all criteria.

outcome_3.result: Cannot claim ownership through adverse possession.
outcome_3.explanation: Possession was with the consent of the true owner, which negates adverse possession.
outcome_3.next_steps: Reassess the nature of possession or seek legal advice.

outcome_4.result: Can claim ownership through adverse possession.
outcome_4.explanation: All legal requirements for adverse possession have been satisfied.
outcome_4.next_steps: File a legal claim to formalize ownership through the courts.

outcome_5.result: Cannot claim ownership through adverse possession.
outcome_5.explanation: Possession was not exclusive, failing a key requirement.
outcome_5.next_steps: Ensure possession is exclusive and reassess after meeting all criteria.

purpose: This decision tree helps determine whether a co-owned property in Sri Lanka can be partitioned through legal proceedings.
entry_question: Is the property co-owned by multiple parties?

start.question: Is the property co-owned by multiple parties?
start.legal_basis: Partition Law of Sri Lanka, Partition Act No. 21 of 1977

node_2.question: Do all co-owners agree to the partition?
node_2.legal_basis: Partition Law of Sri Lanka, Section 2

node_3.question: Is there a dispute among co-owners regarding the partition?
node_3.legal_basis: Partition Law of Sri Lanka, Section 4

node_4.question: Has an attempt been made to resolve the dispute through mediation?
node_4.legal_basis: Mediation (Special Categories of Disputes) Act No. 21 of 2003

outcome_1.explanation: Partition is only applicable to co-owned properties.
outcome_1.next_steps: Consider other legal options for property management or sale.

outcome_2.result: Partition can proceed by mutual agreement
outcome_2.explanation: All co-owners agree to the partition, allowing for a consensual division.
outcome_2.next_steps: Proceed with drafting a partition deed and seek legal assistance to formalize the agreement.

outcome_3.explanation: There is no dispute among co-owners, allowing for a straightforward partition process.
outcome_3.next_steps: File a partition action in court to formalize the division.

outcome_4.explanation: Dispute remains unresolved after mediation, requiring court intervention.
outcome_4.next_steps: File a partition action in court and present evidence of attempted mediation.

outcome_5.explanation: Mediation has not been attempted, which is a prerequisite before court action.
outcome_5.next_steps: Engage in mediation to attempt resolution before proceeding to court.

purpose: Determine the validity and enforceability of a mortgage agreement
entry_question: Is there a valid mortgage agreement in place?

start.question: Is the mortgage agreement in writing and signed by both parties?

node_2.question: Has the mortgage been registered with the relevant land registry?

node_3.question: Is there a default in payment by the mortgagor?

node_4.question: Has the mortgagee issued a notice of default to the mortgagor?

outcome_1.explanation: A mortgage must be in writing and signed by both parties to be valid.
outcome_1.next_steps: Consider drafting a new mortgage agreement that meets legal requirements.

outcome_2.explanation: A mortgage must be registered to be enforceable against third parties.
outcome_2.next_steps: Register the mortgage with the land registry to perfect the security interest.

outcome_3.explanation: The mortgagor is not in default, so the mortgagee cannot enforce the security.
outcome_3.next_steps: Continue monitoring payments and compliance with the mortgage terms.

outcome_4.explanation: The mortgagee can proceed with enforcement actions due to default and proper notice.
outcome_4.next_steps: Consider initiating foreclosure proceedings or negotiating a settlement.

outcome_5.explanation: The mortgagee must issue a notice of default before enforcement.
outcome_5.next_steps: Issue a formal notice of default to the mortgagor and allow time for remedy.

purpose: Determine if a tenancy agreement can be legally terminated by the landlord
entry_question: Can the landlord terminate the tenancy agreement?

start.question: Is there a written lease agreement?

node_2.question: Does the lease agreement specify conditions for termination?

node_3.question: Has the tenant breached any statutory obligations?

node_4.question: Has the tenant violated any of these conditions?

outcome_1.explanation: Without specified conditions, termination is not straightforward
outcome_1.next_steps: Consider negotiating terms or seeking legal advice

outcome_2.explanation: Breach of conditions or statutory obligations allows for termination
outcome_2.next_steps: Proceed with legal notice for termination

outcome_3.explanation: No breach of statutory obligations found
outcome_3.next_steps: Maintain the tenancy or seek mediation

outcome_4.next_steps: Review lease terms or consult a lawyer

purpose: Determine the eligibility for obtaining a grant or lease of state land in Sri Lanka
entry_question: Are you applying for a grant or lease of state land?

start.question: Is the land classified as state land?

node_2.question: Is the land intended for agricultural purposes?

node_3.question: Do you have prior approval from the Divisional Secretary?
node_3.legal_basis: Circulars issued by the Ministry of Lands

node_4.question: Is the land intended for residential or commercial use?

node_5.question: Have you submitted a development plan approved by the Urban Development Authority?
node_5.legal_basis: Urban Development Authority Law No. 41 of 1978

outcome_1.result: Not eligible for state land grant or lease
outcome_1.explanation: The land is not classified as state land.

outcome_2.result: Eligible for state land grant or lease
outcome_2.explanation: All necessary approvals and classifications are met.
outcome_2.next_steps: Proceed with the application process through the Divisional Secretariat.

outcome_3.result: Not eligible due to lack of necessary approvals
outcome_3.explanation: Required approvals from relevant authorities are missing.
outcome_3.next_steps: Obtain the necessary approvals and reapply.

outcome_4.result: Not eligible for current intended use
outcome_4.explanation: The intended use does not align with available state land purposes.
outcome_4.next_steps: Reassess the intended use or explore alternative land options.

purpose: This decision tree helps determine if a foreign national can own property in Sri Lanka.
entry_question: Is the property being purchased residential or commercial?

start.question: Is the property being purchased residential?
start.legal_basis: Land (Restrictions on Alienation) Act, No. 38 of 2014

node_2.question: Is the foreign national purchasing the property through a Sri Lankan company?
node_2.legal_basis: Land (Restrictions on Alienation) Act, No. 38 of 2014

node_3.question: Is the property being purchased for a project approved by the Board of Investment of Sri Lanka?
node_3.legal_basis: Board of Investment of Sri Lanka Law, No. 4 of 1978

outcome_1.result: Foreign national can own the property through a company.
outcome_1.explanation: Foreign nationals can own property if purchased through a company incorporated in Sri Lanka with at least 50% local ownership.
outcome_1.next_steps: Ensure compliance with company ownership requirements and register the property accordingly.

outcome_2.result: Foreign national cannot directly own residential property.
outcome_2.explanation: Direct ownership of residential property by foreign nationals is restricted.
outcome_2.next_steps: Consider alternative ownership structures such as leasing or forming a joint venture with a local partner.

outcome_3.result: Foreign national can own the property.
outcome_3.explanation: Properties for projects approved by the Board of Investment may be exempt from certain restrictions.
outcome_3.next_steps: Proceed with the purchase ensuring compliance with BOI regulations.

outcome_4.result: Foreign national cannot own the property.
outcome_4.explanation: Without BOI approval, foreign nationals face restrictions on owning commercial property.
outcome_4.next_steps: Explore options such as leasing or seeking BOI approval for the project.
